result:
**REST** — simpler, more mature, better for caching and CDNs, each endpoint returns fixed data.

**GraphQL** — clients query exactly what they need, single endpoint, no over/under-fetching, steeper learning curve.

| Tradeoff | REST | GraphQL |
|----------|------|---------|
| **Learning curve** | Low, standard HTTP verbs | Steeper, new query language |
| **Caching** | Native HTTP caching (browsers, CDNs) | Requires custom strategies, usually POST |
| **Over/under-fetching** | Common (fixed response shapes) | Avoided (client specifies fields) |
| **Server complexity** | Simple (each endpoint handles one thing) | Complex (resolves arbitrary queries) |
| **Tooling** | Everywhere (any HTTP client works) | Specialized tools, but excellent (Apollo, Relay) |
| **Real-time** | Polling or webhooks | Native subscriptions |
| **Discoverability** | OpenAPI/Swagger, docs-driven | Introspection built-in, self-documenting |
| **Versioning** | URL/header versioning needed | No versioning (add fields, deprecate old ones) |

**Pick REST if:**
- API is simple, mostly reads, resource-oriented
- Heavy caching is important (public APIs, CDN usage)
- Team wants minimal overhead, fast shipping
- Browser clients (fetch/XMLHttpRequest is enough)

**Pick GraphQL if:**
- Multiple clients with different data needs (mobile vs web vs native)
- Complex, interconnected data (lots of joins needed)
- You want to avoid API versioning churn
- Real-time features are planned

**Middle ground:** Start REST, migrate to GraphQL later if clients start requesting overlapping subsets of data. Or use both (GraphQL wraps REST internally).
